title: Treasurer downplays RBA's spending warning
full_text:
Federal and state spending is expected to outpace economic growth and complicate the Reserve Bank's battle against surging inflation, as the treasurer downplayed Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock's warning to avoid untargeted cost-of-living relief for households in next week's budget.
Jim Chalmers on Wednesday repeated his claim that the RBA's decision to lift interest rates for a third time this year was not driven by government spending, instead citing the "hefty price" of war in the Middle East.
The RBA's nine-member monetary policy board on Tuesday lifted the cash rate by a quarter of a percentage point to 4.35 per cent in a decisive 8-1 vote. It was aimed at tackling Australia's surging inflation, which rose to 4.6 per cent in March on the back of higher petrol prices due to the Iran conflict.
In its quarterly comment released on Tuesday, the RBA called out the rise in government spending over recent years, saying it "has been driven by ... spending on social benefits provided in-kind to households".
It said federal and state governments combined would add more heat into the economy this financial year, noting the combined deficit "would widen further in financial year 2025/26".
KPMG chief economist Brendan Rynne said the high and rising level of government spending would "already make Bullock's job hard, and a continuation of that trajectory will make her job impossible".
"We've got the central bank operating in a contraction monetary policy, and we've got government operating in a stimulatory fiscal policy. And between the two of them, we're getting nowhere."
On Tuesday, asked about reports that Chalmers would include a one-off "earned income offset" of between $200 and $300 for all salaried workers as part of next week's budget, Bullock said Australians were "poorer" because of the war, and "there is no way out of that".
"The extent to which government makes up the shortfalls for households by giving them more money, it makes it harder to dampen demand," she said.
The income offset adds to cost-of-living measures already announced by the Albanese government, including the three-month halving of the fuel excise to 23.6¢ and $1 billion in interest-free loans for businesses squeezed by higher fuel costs.
Chalmers countered on ABC Radio National on Wednesday that "government spending wasn't the reason why rates went up yesterday".
He refused to engage in speculation about what further support for households would be included in the budget, and said Bullock was "asked a hypothetical question" about what would happen if his government poured "heaps more fiscal stimulus" into the economy.
But the governor made the comments with knowledge of the outline of the budget, according to Chalmers, who told journalists in Canberra on Tuesday afternoon that he had "spoken to her on multiple occasions" over the past few weeks.
"You'd expect that to be the case. I've run her through all of my budgets ... and that's what I've done on this occasion," he said. "Again, we provide a bit of detail so that the governor can think through some of the same sorts of issues that we are thinking through."
Victorian Premier Jacinta Allan on Wednesday denied that her government's spending was fuelling inflation, saying cost-of-living support announced in the state budget was "essential, not inflationary".
Allan unveiled $18 billion of new spending in Tuesday's budget, including $450 million on free and half-price public transport fares, $760 million on providing a 20 per cent rebate on car registration and $100 million for additional elective surgeries for children.
"I think it is absolutely essential, not inflationary, that people can pay for their grocery bills. I think it's absolutely essential, not inflationary, that mortgage repayments can be made, that rental repayments can be made," Allan said.
"I think we need to look at this in that broader range of both economic metrics, but also remember, behind every one of these numbers is a person, is a family, and there are so many families who are doing it tough, and that's why we've prioritised those people in yesterday's budget."
Speaking in Townsville on Wednesday, Queensland Treasurer David Janetzki said the state would look to provide cost-of-living relief in the June budget, despite Bullock's warning.
"There is no doubt that war in the Middle East has this flow-through effect right across not just the Queensland economy but the world. It has made the budget process more challenging, but we'll continue to work through it to make sure we get the balance right."
Last month, credit ratings agency S&P singled out Queensland and NSW as the states facing potential ratings downgrades if there was "unexpected weakening" in their budget positions.
Challenger chief economist and ex-RBA official Jonathan Kearns said government spending had outpaced economic growth for the past three years, and called for "critical" restraint to shrink the government's spending.
summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers downplayed the Reserve Bank's concerns over government spending contributing to inflation, as the RBA raised interest rates to combat rising inflation driven by external factors.
publication:
Chalmers is going for broke. Will we end up a poorer country?

BUDGET 2026 If Australians believe Jim Chalmers, then this Tuesday’s budget is meant to be the most consequential in decades. In front of more than one million viewers the Treasurer will frame his speech by saying the budget will not be one ...
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: The RBA increased the cash rate to 4.35% to address inflation, which rose to 4.6% in March.
Chalmers argues that government spending is not the primary cause of rising interest rates, attributing it instead to external conflicts.
State governments are planning to implement cost-of-living measures despite warnings from the RBA regarding inflationary pressures.
relevance: Understanding the interplay between government fiscal policy and monetary policy is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing economic risks and opportunities.
